name: Lebanese Manakish
steps: Make a simple dough from the flour, a pinch of salt, and warm water. Rest 20 minutes.
Mix the za'atar with the oil into a paste and spread over the rolled-out dough.
Bake at 425F for 10-12 minutes until the edges are golden.
steps_detailed: Make a soft dough from the flour, baking powder, a pinch of salt, and warm water. Knead briefly and rest 20-30 minutes.
Roll the dough out into a thin round.
Mix the za'atar with the oil into a spreadable paste and spread generously over the dough, scattering with feta if using.
Bake at 425F (220C) for 10-14 minutes until the edges are golden and crisp.
Manakish is a beloved Lebanese breakfast flatbread, sold fresh every morning from bakeries across the country.

name: Lebanese Foul Medammes
steps: Warm the beans with a splash of their liquid.
Mash lightly and stir in the lime juice, garlic, and oil.
Serve with warm bread.
steps_detailed: Warm the beans with a splash of their liquid over medium-low heat, 5-6 minutes.
Mash lightly, leaving some texture, and stir in the lime juice, garlic, and oil.
Top with the diced tomatoes and cilantro, and serve with warm bread - traditionally made with fava beans, foul medammes is a beloved Lebanese breakfast with roots across the wider Levant and Egypt.

name: Lebanese Labneh with Olive Oil

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Plain Organic Greek Yogurt, Plain
ingredients.note: strained overnight, as a labneh stand-in

steps: Strain the yogurt in a cheesecloth-lined sieve overnight in the fridge to thicken it into labneh.
Spread onto a plate, drizzle with the oil, and sprinkle with za'atar.
Serve with warm bread.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Plain Organic Greek Yogurt, Plain
ingredients_full.note: strained overnight, as a labneh stand-in

steps_detailed: Strain the yogurt in a cheesecloth-lined sieve set over a bowl, refrigerated overnight, until thick and spreadable, resembling labneh.
Spread onto a plate, making a shallow well in the center, and drizzle generously with the oil.
Sprinkle with za'atar and serve with olives and warm bread - labneh is a breakfast fixture across Lebanon.

name: Lebanese Tabbouleh

ingredients.note: cup, chopped, as a parsley stand-in

steps: Soak the bulgur in hot water for 15-20 minutes until softened, then drain well.
Toss with the tomatoes, chopped herbs, and lime juice.
Chill before serving.

ingredients_full.note: cup, chopped, as a parsley stand-in

steps_detailed: Soak the bulgur in hot water for 15-20 minutes until softened, then drain thoroughly and squeeze out excess moisture.
Toss with the tomatoes, herbs (traditionally a large amount of finely chopped parsley, with bulgur almost an accent), onion, lime juice, and oil.
Chill at least 20 minutes before serving - tabbouleh is a Lebanese herb salad, often mistakenly thought of as a grain salad when it's truly meant to be herb-forward.

name: Lebanese Fattoush
steps: Toss the cabbage, cucumber, and tomatoes with the lime juice.
Top with crumbled toasted bread or pita chips if you have them.
Serve chilled.
steps_detailed: Toss the shredded cabbage, cucumber, and tomatoes with the lime juice, sumac, and oil.
Top with crumbled toasted flatbread for the traditional crunch.
Chill briefly and serve - fattoush's tangy sumac dressing and toasted bread pieces make it a distinctly Lebanese salad.

name: Lebanese Kafta

ingredients.note: cup, chopped, as a parsley stand-in

steps: Mix the beef with the grated onion, herbs, and pepper.
Shape into small logs and grill or pan-fry, 4-5 minutes per side, until cooked through.
Serve with rice or flatbread.

ingredients_full.note: cup, chopped, as a parsley stand-in

steps_detailed: Mix the beef thoroughly with the grated onion, herbs, garlic, pepper, and cinnamon (a traditional warm-spice touch).
Shape into small logs around skewers if you have them, or simply as patties.
Grill or pan-fry, 4-6 minutes per side, until well browned and cooked through.
Serve with rice or flatbread and a squeeze of lime - kafta is a Lebanese grilled meat staple, found at nearly every family gathering.

name: Lebanese Shish Taouk

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Plain Organic Greek Yogurt, Plain

steps: Marinate the chicken with the yogurt, garlic, and lime juice for at least 20 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill until charred and cooked through, about 10-12 minutes.
Serve with garlic sauce and flatbread.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Plain Organic Greek Yogurt, Plain

steps_detailed: Marinate the cubed chicken with the yogurt, garlic, lime juice, turmeric, and oil for at least 30 minutes, or overnight for deeper flavor - the yogurt tenderizes the chicken.
Thread onto skewers and grill over medium-high heat, turning occasionally, 12-15 minutes until well charred and cooked through.
Serve with toum (garlic sauce) and warm flatbread - shish taouk is a beloved Lebanese grilled chicken skewer, found at nearly every Lebanese restaurant and street stall.

name: Lebanese Kibbeh
steps: Soak the bulgur in hot water 15 minutes, then drain and mix with half the beef, half the onion, and the cinnamon into a firm shell mixture.
Brown the remaining beef and onion for the filling.
Shape the shell mixture around the filling into small torpedo shapes and bake at 375F for 25-30 minutes.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt
ingredients_full.note: chopped, as a pine nut stand-in

steps_detailed: Soak the bulgur in hot water for 15-20 minutes, then drain thoroughly and squeeze dry.
Blend or pound half the beef with the soaked bulgur, half the onion, and the cinnamon into a smooth, pliable shell mixture.
Brown the remaining beef and onion with the chopped peanuts for the filling.
Shape the shell mixture around a spoonful of filling into small torpedo shapes, sealing completely.
Fry in the oil until deeply golden on all sides, or bake at 375F (190C) for 28-35 minutes - kibbeh is widely considered Lebanon's national dish.

name: Lebanese Chicken Shawarma
steps: Marinate the chicken with the turmeric, garlic, and lime juice for at least 20 minutes.
Pan-fry over high heat, 5-6 minutes, until well browned and cooked through.
Serve with flatbread and a garlic sauce.
steps_detailed: Marinate the thinly sliced chicken with the turmeric, garlic, lime juice, cinnamon, and oil for at least 30 minutes, or overnight.
Cook over high heat in a wide skillet, 6-8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until deeply browned and slightly crisp at the edges, mimicking a shawarma spit's charred exterior.
Serve with warm flatbread, pickles, and garlic sauce - shawarma is a Lebanese street food icon, now beloved worldwide.

name: Lebanese Stuffed Eggplant (Sheikh el Mahshi)
steps: Roast the eggplant halves at 400F for 15-18 minutes until softened.
Brown the beef with the onion, then spoon into the eggplant halves.
Top with the diced tomatoes and bake 15 more minutes.
steps_detailed: Roast the eggplant halves, cut-side up, at 400F (200C) for 18-22 minutes until softened and starting to collapse.
Brown the beef with the onion, garlic, and cinnamon, 8-10 minutes.
Scoop a little of the eggplant flesh into the meat mixture, then spoon everything back into the eggplant shells.
Top with the diced tomatoes and bake 15-18 more minutes - a comforting Lebanese stuffed vegetable dish.

name: Lebanese Freekeh with Chicken
steps: Brown the chicken with the onion.
Add the bulgur and broth, and simmer, covered, 18-20 minutes until the grain is tender and the chicken is cooked through.
Serve hot.
steps_detailed: Brown the chicken with the onion, garlic, and cinnamon over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the bulgur (standing in for freekeh, a smoky roasted green wheat) and chicken broth.
Cover and simmer 18-22 minutes until the grain is tender and the chicken is cooked through, with most of the liquid absorbed.
Top with the chopped walnuts and serve - freekeh is a treasured, distinctly smoky grain in Lebanese cooking, often reserved for special meals.

name: Lebanese Hummus

ingredients.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids
Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps: Blend the chickpeas, tahini, lime juice, and garlic with a splash of water until smooth.
Drizzle with olive oil and serve with warm flatbread.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids
Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps_detailed: Blend the chickpeas, tahini, lime juice, garlic, and oil with a splash of water until very smooth and creamy, scraping down the sides as needed.
Spread onto a plate, drizzle with more oil, and serve with warm flatbread - Lebanon and its neighbors have long disputed hummus's exact origins, but it remains central to Lebanese mezze.

name: Lebanese Baba Ghanoush

ingredients.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps: Roast the whole eggplant at 425F for 25-30 minutes until completely soft and collapsed.
Scoop out the flesh and mash with the tahini, lime juice, and garlic.
Serve with flatbread.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps_detailed: Roast the whole eggplant at 425F (220C) for 28-35 minutes, turning occasionally, until the skin is charred and the inside is completely soft.
Let cool slightly, then scoop out the flesh and mash with the tahini, lime juice, garlic, and oil until mostly smooth with some texture remaining.
Serve with warm flatbread - baba ghanoush's smoky depth comes from the direct charring of the eggplant skin.

name: Lebanese Za'atar Roasted Chickpeas

ingredients.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids

steps: Toss the chickpeas with the oil and za'atar.
Roast at 425F for 20-25 minutes, shaking occasionally, until crisp.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, canned, drained solids

steps_detailed: Pat the chickpeas very dry with a towel.
Toss with the oil and za'atar.
Roast at 425F (220C) for 22-28 minutes, shaking the pan occasionally, until deeply golden and crisp - a simple, addictive Lebanese snack.

name: Lebanese Warak Enab (Stuffed Grape Leaves)
steps: Mix the uncooked rice with the tomatoes and a little lime juice for the filling.
Wrap small portions in the grape leaves, rolling tightly.
Simmer in water to cover for 35-40 minutes until the rice is tender.

ingredients_full.note: cup, chopped, as a parsley stand-in

steps_detailed: Mix the uncooked rice with the tomatoes, herbs, lime juice, and oil for the filling.
Lay each grape leaf flat, place a spoonful of filling near the stem end, fold in the sides, and roll tightly into a small cylinder.
Arrange the rolls snugly in a pot, cover with water, and simmer 35-45 minutes until the rice is fully tender.
Serve warm or at room temperature - warak enab, stuffed grape leaves, are a beloved Lebanese mezze staple.

name: Lebanese Rosewater Rice Pudding
steps: Simmer the rice in the milk with the sugar for 20-25 minutes, stirring often, until thick.
Stir in the rosewater off the heat.
Chill before serving.
steps_detailed: Simmer the rice in the milk with the sugar over medium-low heat, stirring often, 22-28 minutes, until thick and creamy.
Stir in the rosewater off the heat, adding gradually and tasting, since it's potent.
Chill and top with the chopped pistachios before serving - a fragrant, beloved Lebanese dessert.

name: Lebanese Date and Walnut Bites
steps: Blend the dates and walnuts together until they form a sticky, cohesive mixture.
Roll into small balls.
Chill before serving.
steps_detailed: Blend the dates, walnuts, and cinnamon together in a food processor until they form a sticky, well-combined mixture.
Roll into small balls, pressing firmly so they hold together.
Chill at least 20 minutes before serving - a simple, naturally sweet Lebanese treat.

name: Lebanese Maamoul
steps: Mix the flour, butter, and sugar into a soft, crumbly dough.
Flatten portions, fill with the mashed dates, and reshape into small rounds.
Bake at 350F for 18-22 minutes until just set, not browned.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, butter, and sugar into a soft, slightly crumbly dough, being careful not to overwork it.
Mash the dates with the rosewater until they form a smooth paste.
Flatten a portion of dough, place a spoonful of the date paste in the center, and reshape around it, sealing completely, then press gently into a small mold or simply flatten by hand.
Bake at 350F (175C) for 18-24 minutes until just set and very pale - maamoul should not brown.
A beloved Lebanese holiday cookie, especially associated with Eid celebrations.

name: Lebanese Toum (Garlic Sauce)
steps: Blend the garlic with the lime juice, then slowly drizzle in the oil while blending until thick and creamy.
Serve alongside shawarma or grilled meats.
steps_detailed: Blend the garlic with a little of the lime juice until very finely pureed.
With the blender running, slowly drizzle in the oil, alternating with the remaining lime juice, until the mixture emulsifies into a thick, fluffy, mayonnaise-like sauce.
This is a simplified version of toum, which traditionally takes real patience to whip into its signature airy texture - serve alongside shawarma, grilled chicken, or vegetables.

name: Lebanese Tahini Lemon Sauce

ingredients.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

steps: Whisk the tahini, lime juice, and garlic together with a splash of water until smooth and pourable.
Serve over grilled vegetables, falafel, or fish.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Seeds, sesame butter, tahini, from roasted and toasted kernels (most common type)

ingredients_full.note: cup, chopped, as a parsley stand-in

steps_detailed: Whisk the tahini, lime juice, and garlic together with a splash of water, adding water gradually until it reaches a smooth, pourable consistency - the mixture will seize up before it loosens, so keep whisking.
Stir in the chopped herbs.
Serve over grilled vegetables, falafel, or fish - this tahini sauce, called tarator in some regions, is endlessly versatile in Lebanese cooking.
